petitioner_background: The petitioner is a folk guitarist/singer, apparently of Ukrainian/Russian background, who performs Russian and Ukrainian contemporary and historical folk music. He sought classification as an alien of extraordinary ability in the arts under section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Act. He had previously been approved for a P-1 nonimmigrant visa and several P-3 nonimmigrant visas, and was working as the main performer at the Sugar Rush Cafe, a Russian nightclub/restaurant in south Florida. He had also previously filed a Form I-140 for the same classification based on essentially the same arguments and evidence.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted: (1) a Certificate from the 2nd International Children's Competition Festival for Folklore Ensemble, Kozaki From Podille (unspecified date); (2) a Certificate of Honour from the Vinnitsa Children's Musical School for First Place in the 4th School Technique Competition of a Piano Kind, Young Virtuoso (unspecified date); (3) a Diploma from the Ministry of Culture of Russian Federation for 3rd Degree for the 4th All-Russia Competition of Young Jazz Musicians on May 4-7, 1991; and (4) a Diploma from an unnamed source for an unknown award for Fireworks at the 3rd International Jazz Review Concert of the Students of Musical Schools and Lycee (unspecified date).
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that item 1 failed to provide the petitioner's placement at the competition, and item 4 failed to provide placement and the actual awarding entity. The petitioner failed to provide information establishing that the competitions were at the national or international level — simply having 'International' or 'National' in the title does not elevate the award. Without documentary evidence about the competitions themselves (level of participants, selection criteria, prestige), the AAO could not conclude the awards were nationally or internationally recognized. Additionally, the awards were restricted to children, students, and 'young jazz musicians,' and such age/non-professional-restricted competitions do not indicate the petitioner is in the small percentage at the very top of the field, citing Matter of Price and the Racine reasoning. The petitioner also failed to show the awards were for singing (his claimed field). Finally, only item 3 specified a year (1991), which was over 15 years prior to filing, failing to establish sustained acclaim.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted: (1) an extract from an article titled 'Construction of a Studio in Vinnitsa' in Audiostop; (2) an advertisement for the petitioner's compact disc 'Never Ending Story' on the website CD Baby; and (3) an advertisement for the petitioner's compact disc 'Never Ending Story' on the website India Star Search.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: Regarding item 1, the English translation failed to comply with 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3) because only an extract translation was provided instead of a full English language translation, and the petitioner failed to provide the author and date as required by 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(iii). The evidence was therefore not probative. Regarding items 2 and 3, these were advertisements for downloading the petitioner's songs rather than published material about the petitioner, and failed to contain titles, dates, and authors. The AAO also found counsel failed to establish that Audiostop, CD Baby, or Indie Star Search are professional or major trade publications or major media, noting that international accessibility of a website is not by itself a realistic indicator of 'major media.' Two advertisements were insufficient to establish the level of acclaim required.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: On appeal, counsel claimed eligibility based on the petitioner headlining at the Sugar Rush Cafe, the petitioner's songs played on Radio Swanson, and the petitioner's albums and concerts.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO held that the plain language of 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(vii) indicates this criterion is intended for visual artists (such as sculptors and painters) rather than singers. It is inherent to the field of singing to perform on stage, and not every stage performance is an artistic exhibition or showcase. In the performing arts, acclaim is established by attracting substantial audiences, not merely by appearing in public. The petitioner failed to demonstrate that his performances were something other than what is intrinsic to a performer, and the record contained no evidence that his performances or venues were recognized as prestigious. The AAO noted the evidence was more relevant to the commercial success criterion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted letters of recommendation regarding his performances at the Sugar Rush Cafe, describing him as the main performer, a Ukrainian folk guitarist/singer with culturally unique skills, who brought revenue and cultural value to south Florida, performed for thousands of people, and was an excellent, punctual, reliable performer. One letter described Sugar Rush as a Russian restaurant/nightclub specializing in Eastern European clientele and 'by far, the best nightclub specializing in Russian and Ukrainian music in the southern United States.' One letter contained language identical to another letter. The petitioner also submitted a letter simply indicating he worked at the Sugar Rush Cafe.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the letters insufficient because they were all from individuals who had worked or interacted with the petitioner. While such letters can provide details about the petitioner's role, they cannot form the cornerstone of an extraordinary ability claim because sustained national or international acclaim requires evidence beyond immediate acquaintances, citing section 203(b)(1)(A)(i) and Matter of Caron International. Letters solicited for immigration petitions carry less weight than preexisting independent evidence. The documentation did not show the petitioner's position was leading or critical — even the letters indicated Sugar Rush employed other singers and entertainers with similar expertise, and the petitioner failed to establish how his position differentiated him from other performers or management. The AAO was also not persuaded that Sugar Rush Cafe is an establishment with a distinguished reputation; simply submitting letters stating so was insufficient, and no supporting documentation established its reputation as distinct from other nightclubs.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted 17 items: (1) a partial English translation of a radio show 'Rhythm' in Vinnitsa, Ukraine (no foreign language document); (2) a copy of a contract in a foreign language without translation; (3) information from Amazon.com in a foreign language without translation; (4) a partial English translation of a contract between the petitioner and Magnet Production; (5) a partial English translation of a contract with ArtStarsStudioSouz; (6) a partial English translation of a document regarding two of the petitioner's songs; (7) copies of foreign language compact disks and covers without translations; (8) a copy of a compact disk titled 'Anatolij Antonishen & Barhulniy Sezon Group, 90-97 Years'; (9) an advertisement from CD Baby's website for the album 'Never Ending Story' with background on CD Baby; (10) an email from James McCullough of IndieRhythm.com regarding advertising; (11) an advertisement from CDQuest.com; (12) an advertisement from AudioLunchbox.com; (13) an advertisement from Indie Star Search's website; (14) an advertisement from Rhapsody.com; (15) a diploma from the Vinnitsa Region State Administration of the Management of Culture stating the petitioner was an artistic administrator at Vinnitsa Philharmonic Society and member of two bands; (16) a statement from the Department of Culture of the Municipal Palace of Arts stating the petitioner was employed in the musical group The Podol Cossacks; (17) copies of two advertisements for Sugar Rush Cafe with a picture of the petitioner performing; and letters regarding his performances at Sugar Rush Cafe.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The criterion requires evidence of commercial successes in the form of 'sales' or 'receipts.' Regarding items 1-7, the petitioner failed to provide full English language translations in compliance with 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3), so this evidence was given no weight. Regarding items 8-17, while the petitioner established he created at least two compact disks, advertised them on various websites, and performed in musical groups, he failed to submit any documentation reflecting sales of the compact disks or box office receipts for his performances. There was no evidence of documented sales or receipts showing commercial successes consistent with sustained national or international acclaim — no evidence his performances drew record crowds, were regular sell-outs, or drew greater audiences than others, and no evidence his recordings generated substantial national or international sales.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO concurred with the director's finding that the petitioner failed to demonstrate receipt of a major, internationally recognized award, or that he met at least three of the regulatory criteria under 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3). The conclusion from considering each criterion separately was consistent with a review of the evidence in the aggregate — even in the aggregate, the evidence did not distinguish the petitioner as one of the small percentage who has risen to the very top of the field of endeavor. While the petitioner earned the respect and admiration of those offering recommendation letters, he failed to establish a record of accomplishment placing him among that small percentage at the very top of his field, or that his achievements set him significantly above almost all others in his field at a national or international level. The AAO also rejected the argument that prior P-1 and P-3 nonimmigrant approvals required approval of the I-140, noting USCIS is not bound by prior erroneous approvals and I-140 petitions receive more scrutiny than nonimmigrant petitions. The burden of proof under section 291 of the Act was not met, and the appeal was dismissed.

Matter of Price, 20 I&N Dec. 953 (Assoc. Commr. 1994)

Louisiana Philharmonic Orchestra v. INS, 2000 WL 282785 (E.D. La.), aff'd 248 F.3d 1139 (5th Cir. 2001), cert. denied, 122 S.Ct. 51 (2001)
AFM Update: Chapter 22: Employment-based petitions (AD03-01), September 12, 2006 memorandum by Michael Aytes
fraud_or_procedural_issues: The AAO noted that one reference letter contained language identical to another letter, suggesting the letters may have been drafted by the same person rather than reflecting independent assessments. Procedurally, counsel argued the director's RFE and decision failed to provide 'clear guidance' as required by a September 12, 2006 Aytes memorandum, but the AAO rejected this, finding the RFE complied with 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(8). However, the AAO agreed with counsel that the director's decision failed to discuss the documentary evidence as it related to the regulatory criteria, and therefore conducted a de novo review on appeal. The petitioner also repeatedly failed to comply with 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3) by submitting only partial/extract English translations rather than full certified translations of foreign language documents. Additionally, the petitioner had previously filed a Form I-140 with essentially the same arguments and evidence, which had been denied with extensive findings, undermining any claim of lack of notice.
